mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers Taylorism, Fordist mass production, automation anxieties, the service economy transition, and psychological/social aspects of rationalized work -- central to this section's scope.
The Industrial Revolution's factory system, textile manufacturing innovations, and steam power represent the transformation of production organization from craft to machine-based mass production.
Covers factory system growth, working conditions, child labor, trade union movements, and responses including Luddism and Chartism -- directly addressing the social consequences of rationalized work.
Megalithic construction required large-scale social organisation of communal labour for monumental building, relevant to prehistoric and ancient organization of work for large-scale construction.
The Needham Question examines why industrial production methods developed in Europe rather than China, addressing changes in production techniques and the foundations of modern industrial production.
Automata's historical trajectory toward computing and robotics connects to the use of machines as replacements for labour.
The history of the city from Bronze Age to 1800 covers urban manufacturing, craft guilds, and the organization of labour that sustained urban civilization.
